strInstructions: Toast the dried chilies and spices briefly until fragrant, then grind or blend them with shallots, garlic, ginger, turmeric, lemongrass, cilantro roots, shrimp paste, and curry powder to form a thick curry paste. Fry the paste in coconut cream until aromatic and the oil begins to separate. Add chicken drumsticks or thighs and coat them well in the curry paste. Pour in coconut milk and chicken stock, then simmer until the chicken is tender. Season with fish sauce, palm sugar, and soy sauce. Cook egg noodles separately. Place noodles in bowls, ladle the curry and chicken over them, then top with crispy fried noodles, pickled mustard greens, shallots, lime, and chili oil.

In a small bowl, combine the ground pork with 1 tablespoon Golden Mountain sauce or soy sauce and set aside. Pound garlic and Thai chilies in a mortar and pestle until chunky, or finely chop them. Heat oil in a large pan over medium heat, then add the garlic and chilies and stir quickly. Add the ground pork and raise the heat to medium-high. When the pork is halfway cooked, add the remaining Golden Mountain sauce or soy sauce and oyster sauce. Continue stir-frying until the pork is fully cooked. Taste and add sugar if you want a slightly sweet flavor. Turn off the heat and stir in the Thai basil leaves. Serve with steamed rice and a crispy fried egg.
strIngredient2: Golden Mountain Sauce or Soy Sauce

strInstructions: Pound garlic and chilies in a mortar until broken down. Add palm sugar, fish sauce, lime juice, and tamarind juice, then stir until the sugar dissolves. Add long beans and bruise them lightly. Add shredded green papaya, tomatoes, dried shrimp, and roasted peanuts. Pound and toss gently until the papaya absorbs the dressing but remains crisp. Taste and adjust until the salad is sour, salty, sweet, and spicy. Serve immediately.

Cook ground pork in a small pan with a splash of water until fully cooked but still juicy. Remove from heat. Add fish sauce, lime juice, chili flakes, toasted rice powder, shallots, green onions, cilantro, and mint. Toss until well combined. Taste and adjust the seasoning so it is salty, sour, spicy, and aromatic. Serve warm or room temperature with lettuce, cucumber, and sticky rice.

Season the pork scotch steaks, pork butt steaks, or skirt steaks with salt and pepper. Grill the meat to your preferred doneness, or sear it in a very hot cast-iron pan for a few minutes per side. Let the meat rest for about 10 minutes, then slice it thinly and keep the juices. Add the sliced meat back to the pan with water or broth, lime juice, fish sauce, roasted rice powder, Thai roasted chili powder, and sugar. Toss over medium heat until the sauce thickens slightly and the flavors are balanced. Turn off the heat, then add sliced shallots, green onion, cilantro, and mint. Serve immediately with sticky rice or fresh vegetables.
strIngredient1: Pork Scotch Steaks or Pork Butt or Skirt Steaks

strInstructions: Cut pork belly and pork shoulder into chunks. Fry curry paste with a little oil until aromatic. Add the pork and stir until coated. Add water, tamarind paste, palm sugar, soy sauce, fish sauce, pickled garlic, ginger, and hung lay curry powder. Simmer slowly until the pork is tender and the sauce becomes thick and glossy. Adjust the balance so the curry is sweet, sour, salty, and warmly spiced. Serve with rice.

Make the curry paste by pounding kosher salt and lemongrass in a mortar until coarse. Add shallots and garlic and pound again, then add Thai chilies and makrut lime leaves and pound into a coarse paste. Add the chicken to a pot with the paste and water or stock, then bring to a simmer. Cook until the chicken is nearly done. Add vegetables and herbs such as dill and continue simmering until everything is cooked. Season with fish sauce and fermented fish sauce if using. Serve hot with sticky rice or jasmine rice.

Fry jungle curry paste in oil until fragrant. Add chicken pieces and stir until the outside changes color. Add water or chicken stock and bring to a boil. Add Thai eggplants, bamboo shoots, long beans, green peppercorns, and makrut lime leaves. Simmer until the vegetables are tender and the chicken is cooked. Season with fish sauce and a little palm sugar. Finish with Thai basil and serve with jasmine rice.

Make the sour curry broth by simmering gaeng som curry paste in water. Season with tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar until sour, salty, and slightly sweet. Beat eggs with chopped cha om leaves, then fry into an omelet and cut into squares. Add shrimp to the curry broth and cook until just done. Add the cha om omelet pieces and simmer briefly so they absorb the soup. Serve hot with jasmine rice.

Heat oil in a wok and fry red curry paste until fragrant and slightly darker. Add sliced pork and stir-fry until cooked. Add long beans and toss until tender-crisp. Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Add finely shredded makrut lime leaves and toss again. The dish should be dry, glossy, and intensely aromatic. Serve with jasmine rice.

Combine minced garlic, minced cilantro stems, palm sugar, fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, white pepper, and MSG if using. Slice the pork butt thinly and mix it thoroughly with the marinade. Let the pork marinate until well seasoned. Thread the pork onto skewers, packing the slices closely together. Grill over medium heat, turning often, until the pork is cooked through, caramelized, and lightly charred. Serve with cilantro leaves and sticky rice.

Pound garlic, cilantro roots, white pepper, and lemongrass into a paste. Mix with fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, palm sugar, and turmeric. Rub the marinade over the chicken and marinate for several hours or overnight. Grill over medium heat, turning often, until the chicken is cooked through and the skin is golden. Serve with sticky rice and dipping sauce.

Poach chicken gently with ginger, garlic, cilantro roots, and salt until cooked through. Reserve the broth. Fry chicken fat or oil with garlic and ginger, then add jasmine rice and toast briefly. Cook the rice with the reserved chicken broth until fragrant. Make the dipping sauce with fermented soybean paste, ginger, garlic, chilies, vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, and lime juice. Slice the chicken and serve over the rice with cucumber, cilantro, broth, and sauce.

Warm oil in a large pot or wok over medium-high heat. Add the pork leg and brown it on all sides for about 5 minutes. Pound coriander roots, garlic, and black peppercorns into a rough paste. In a large pot over medium heat, fry the paste with cinnamon sticks, star anise, and soaked dried shiitake mushrooms for about 30 seconds until fragrant. Add palm sugar and stir until it begins to caramelize. Pour in water, then add Chinese five-spice powder, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, salt, and the browned pork leg. Cover and simmer on low heat for about 3 hours until the pork is fall-apart tender. Add boiled eggs during the last 30 minutes if using. Serve the braised pork leg over jasmine rice with the braising sauce, pickled mustard greens, and blanched choy sum or similar greens.

Simmer beef bones or beef stock with cinnamon, star anise, galangal, cilantro roots, garlic, and soy sauces until deeply flavored. Braise beef until tender, then slice. Season the broth with fish sauce, sugar, and a small amount of beef blood if using. Cook rice noodles briefly in boiling water, then place them in bowls. Top with sliced beef, meatballs, bean sprouts, herbs, and broth. Serve with chili vinegar, fish sauce, sugar, and chili flakes.

In a small bowl, mix fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. Keep the sauce near the stove with the chicken and prepared vegetables. Heat a wok or large skillet over high heat until very hot. Add the oil and swirl to coat the pan. Add the chicken in an even layer and sear undisturbed for about 1 minute until lightly browned but not fully cooked. Add the red bell pepper, onion, scallions, ginger, garlic, and prepared sauce. Stir-fry for 3 to 4 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the vegetables are crisp-tender. Serve immediately with cooked white or brown rice.
strIngredient5: Boneless Skinless Chicken Breast or Thigh

strInstructions: Prepare the glass noodles according to the packet instructions, then drain and rinse briefly so they do not stick together. Toss the sliced chicken thighs with Maggi seasoning or light soy sauce and a pinch of white pepper. In a small bowl, mix oyster sauce, fish sauce, Maggi seasoning or soy sauce, oil, sugar, and white pepper to make the sauce. Heat oil in a large nonstick pan over high heat. Add onion and stir briefly, then add the chicken and cook until almost done. Add garlic and the Chinese broccoli stems, then stir-fry until the chicken is cooked through. Push everything to one side, add a little oil, and scramble the eggs. Add the Chinese broccoli leaves, prepared glass noodles, and sauce. Toss with two spatulas until the noodles absorb the sauce and everything is evenly mixed. Serve immediately.
strMeasure1: 250 g or 8 oz, prepared per packet
strIngredient8: Maggi Seasoning or Light Soy Sauce
strMeasure8: 2 tsp for chicken plus 1 tbsp for sauce
strMeasure9: pinch for chicken plus 1/8 tsp for sauce

strInstructions: Marinate sliced pork with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, cornstarch, and oil. Char fresh wide rice noodles in a hot wok with dark soy sauce, then set aside. Stir-fry garlic and marinated pork until partly cooked. Add Chinese broccoli and stock. Season with soy sauce and fermented soybean paste. Thicken with tapioca or cornstarch slurry to make a glossy gravy. Pour the gravy over the noodles and serve with chili vinegar.

Combine rice flour, cornstarch, and water in a bowl and stir until smooth. Season with salt and pepper. Fold in the drained mussels and chopped spring onions. Heat vegetable oil in a large frying pan or wok over medium-high heat. Pour the batter into the pan and spread it into a thin pancake. Cook undisturbed until the edges become crisp and golden. Flip and cook the other side. Crack the eggs directly onto the pancake, spread them evenly, and cook until set. Transfer to a plate, garnish with extra spring onions, and serve with sweet chili sauce.

Simmer fish with lemongrass, galangal, and makrut lime leaves until cooked, then flake the fish. Pound or blend the fish with red curry paste until smooth. Simmer coconut milk with the curry-fish mixture until aromatic. Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Serve the thick curry sauce over fermented rice noodles with bean sprouts, cucumber, long beans, cabbage, basil, and boiled eggs.

Prepare the curry paste by pounding or blending dried chilies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, shrimp paste, and salt. Simmer pork ribs in water or stock until tender. Fry the curry paste until fragrant, then add ground pork and cook until no longer pink. Add tomatoes and cook until softened. Add the pork rib broth, pork ribs, dried cotton tree flowers, and fermented soybean discs if using. Simmer until rich and flavorful. Season with fish sauce and salt. Serve the broth over khanom jeen rice noodles with cabbage, bean sprouts, cilantro, green onions, lime, and fried garlic.

Make the chicken marinade by combining plain yogurt, ground turmeric, ground cumin, ground coriander, ground cinnamon, onion paste, garlic paste, ginger paste, minced cilantro roots or stems, fish sauce, salt, white pepper, and black pepper. Coat the boneless chicken thighs with the marinade and let them rest for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor. Rinse jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then place it in a rice cooker. Add chicken broth or water, onion paste, garlic paste, ginger paste, ground turmeric, salt, and cherry tomatoes if using. Layer the marinated chicken thighs over the rice and pour over any remaining marinade. Cook on the regular rice cooker setting. While it cooks, mix Thai sweet chili sauce with water and chopped cilantro for the sauce. When done, let the rice sit for 10 minutes, remove the chicken, fluff the rice, then serve with fried shallots, cilantro, cucumber slices, and the sweet chili sauce.
strMeasure3: 1 tsp for marinade plus 1 tsp for rice
strMeasure7: 1 tbsp for marinade plus 1 tbsp for rice
strMeasure8: 1 tbsp for marinade plus 1 tsp for rice
strMeasure9: 1 tbsp for marinade plus 1 tsp for rice
strIngredient10: Cilantro or Coriander Roots and Stems
strMeasure12: 1/4 tsp for marinade plus 1/2 tsp for rice

strInstructions: Mix oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, fish sauce, white sugar, and water in a small bowl. Heat oil in a wok or large skillet over medium heat. Add the raw cashews and cook for about 5 minutes until dark golden and crunchy, then remove with a slotted spoon. Turn the heat to high and add garlic and onion. Cook for about 30 seconds. Add the chicken and stir-fry for about 1 minute until the outside changes from pink to white. Add the white parts of the green onions and the sliced red cayenne pepper, then cook until the chicken is just cooked through. Pour in the sauce and cook until it reduces into a syrupy coating. Add the green parts of the green onions and the toasted cashews. Toss briefly and serve with jasmine rice, garnished with extra red chili if desired.

For the chili paste, pound the whole dried chilies in a mortar and pestle. Add sliced lemongrass, black peppercorns, uncooked rice, garlic, kaffir lime peel, turmeric if using, and salt, then pound into a fine paste. Add shrimp paste at the end and pound until blended. For the soup, heat 2 cups of water in a wok or pot, add the tai pla sauce, and bring it back to a boil. Stir well, then strain the liquid to remove any bits of fish. Return the strained liquid to the wok. Add the chili paste. Add 1/2 cup water to the mortar, stir to loosen the remaining paste, then pour it into the wok. Add Thai eggplants and bamboo shoots, cover, and cook for about 5 minutes. Remove the lid, add green beans if using, dried fish, and sugar. Add shredded kaffir lime leaves and lime juice, stir, remove from heat, and serve with jasmine rice.
strMeasure9: large chunks of 1 whole dried fish

strInstructions: Soak glass noodles until pliable, then drain. Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and stock for the sauce. Layer ginger, garlic, cilantro roots, peppercorns, and pork belly or bacon at the bottom of a pot. Add shrimp, glass noodles, and sauce. Cover and cook until the shrimp are done and the noodles absorb the sauce. Garnish with celery and cilantro.

Pat the salmon dry and season lightly. Fry or pan-sear until the outside is crisp and the fish is cooked through. In a separate pan, fry garlic and chilies until fragrant. Add sweet chili sauce, fish sauce, tamarind or lime juice, and sugar if needed. Simmer until the sauce is glossy and slightly thick. Pour the sweet spicy chili sauce over the crispy salmon and garnish with herbs before serving.
searchAliases: salmon with sweet spicy chili sauce

strInstructions: Mix the dressing by combining roasted chili paste, palm sugar, fish sauce, and lime juice until the sugar dissolves. Prepare the herbs by chopping the lettuce, mint, and cilantro. Slice the shallots or red onion very thinly. Remove the tough outer layers of the lemongrass and slice the tender lower portion very thinly. If using makrut lime leaves, slice them finely. Peel and devein the shrimp, brush them lightly with oil, and grill until cooked through, about 2 to 3 minutes depending on size. Combine the herbs and lettuce in a bowl, add some of the dressing, then add the hot grilled shrimp. Toss gently, add more dressing to taste, and serve immediately.

Soak glass noodles until soft, then blanch briefly and drain. Cook shrimp and ground pork separately until done. Mix lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, garlic, and chilies into a sharp dressing. Toss the noodles with shrimp, pork, tomatoes, celery, onion, cilantro, and the dressing. Serve immediately while the noodles are still bouncy and the salad is bright.

Grill or sear the beef until cooked to your preferred doneness, then rest before slicing thinly. Make the dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, garlic, and chilies. Toss the sliced beef with cucumber, tomatoes, red onion, cilantro, mint, and the dressing. Taste and adjust until the salad is sour, salty, spicy, and slightly sweet. Serve immediately.

Mix fish paste with red curry paste, egg, fish sauce, sugar, sliced long beans, and finely shredded makrut lime leaves until sticky and well combined. Wet your hands and shape the mixture into small patties. Heat oil in a pan and fry the fish cakes until puffed, firm, and golden. Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with cucumber relish or sweet chili sauce.
strIngredient9: Cucumber Relish or Sweet Chili Sauce

strInstructions: Finely process shrimp with pork fat or egg white, garlic, white pepper, soy sauce, sugar, and cornstarch until sticky. Chill the mixture until firm. Shape into patties, coat in breadcrumbs, and deep-fry until golden and crisp. Drain well and serve with plum sauce or sweet chili sauce.

Crack the eggs into a bowl and beat them with minced pork, fish sauce, soy sauce, and a little black pepper until well combined. Heat plenty of oil in a wok until very hot. Pour the egg mixture into the hot oil so it puffs up and becomes crispy. Fry until golden on one side, then flip and cook the other side. Drain briefly and serve immediately with hot jasmine rice and chili sauce.

Hard-boil the eggs and peel them. Pound garlic, cilantro roots, and white pepper into a paste. Fry the paste in oil until aromatic, then add pork belly and brown lightly. Add palm sugar and cook until caramelized. Add water, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, cinnamon, star anise, tofu puffs, and boiled eggs. Simmer until the pork is tender and the eggs are deeply colored. Serve with rice.

Make a paste with garlic, cilantro roots, and white pepper. Fry the paste until aromatic, then add pork belly and brown it lightly. Add palm sugar and cook until caramelized. Add water, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, five-spice, cinnamon, star anise, and hard-boiled eggs. Simmer until the pork is tender and the eggs are deeply colored. Add fried tofu if using and continue simmering until the broth is rich. Serve with rice.
searchAliases: thai pork belly stew with eggs

strInstructions: Slice the chosen meat into thin strips or bite-size pieces. Marinate it with oyster sauce, soy sauce, oil, and egg white for about 30 minutes, keeping the egg yolk for the hot pot. Prepare the glass noodles and vegetables. Heat water in a hot pot or slow cooker. Add mushrooms, corn, pumpkin, or carrots first and cook until softened. Season with soy sauce and salt. Add the marinated meat and egg yolk, then add the remaining vegetables. Cook until the meat and vegetables are done. Serve with Thai suki sauce, extra chilies, and coriander.

Prepare cooked jasmine rice or butterfly pea rice. Make the dressing by simmering palm sugar, water, tamarind, fish sauce or soy sauce, and aromatics until thickened. Arrange rice with shredded lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, bean sprouts, long beans, cucumber, green mango, herbs, toasted coconut, and dried shrimp or vegetarian substitute. Drizzle with dressing and toss before eating.

Cook ground chicken in a pan with a splash of water until fully cooked but still moist. Remove from heat. Add fish sauce, lime juice, chili flakes, toasted rice powder, shallots, green onions, cilantro, and mint. Toss well and adjust seasoning to taste. Serve with lettuce, cucumber, and sticky rice.

Make a curry paste by pounding dried chilies, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, garlic, shallots, makrut lime zest, cilantro roots, and shrimp paste. Mix the paste with ground pork, fish sauce, and finely shredded makrut lime leaves. Stuff into sausage casings or shape into patties. Grill slowly until cooked through and aromatic. Serve with sticky rice and fresh vegetables.

Pound dried chilies, shallots, garlic, and shrimp paste into a coarse chile paste. Heat oil in a skillet and fry the paste until fragrant. Add ground pork and cook, breaking it apart, until no longer pink. Add tomatoes and cook until they collapse into a thick sauce. Season with fish sauce and sugar. Continue simmering until the dip is thick and rich. Serve with pork rinds, cucumber, cabbage, and steamed vegetables.

Roast green chilies, shallots, and garlic until blistered, charred, and softened. Peel the chilies if desired. Pound the roasted chilies, shallots, and garlic together with salt until coarse. Season with fish sauce or soy sauce and lime juice. Taste and adjust the saltiness and acidity. Serve with sticky rice, pork rinds if using, and fresh or steamed vegetables.

Make the sauce by simmering palm sugar, fish sauce or soy sauce, water, galangal, shallots, lemongrass, and shrimp paste or vegetarian substitute until thick and syrupy. Toast coconut until golden. Arrange betel leaves or lettuce with small piles of lime, ginger, shallots, chilies, peanuts, toasted coconut, and dried shrimp if using. Spoon a little sauce into each leaf, fold into a small bite, and eat immediately.

Heat a wok over medium heat and add the pork. Stir-fry until the pork starts to release fat. Add southern Thai dry curry paste and stir constantly so the paste coats the pork evenly. Continue frying until the pork is cooked and the mixture becomes dry and fragrant. Season with fish sauce and sugar if needed. Add finely sliced makrut lime leaves and chilies, then stir briefly. Serve hot with rice and fresh vegetables.

Prepare the shrimp by removing the heads, peeling the shells, and deveining them. Peel the stink beans from their pods and remove the inner skin. Heat oil in a wok over medium heat, then add southern Thai curry paste and shrimp paste. Stir-fry until fragrant. Add the shrimp and stir-fry until they begin turning pink. Add the stink beans and toss briefly. Season with oyster sauce and sugar, adding a splash of water if the curry paste becomes dry. Tear in the kaffir lime leaves and stir-fry for a few seconds. Serve immediately with hot rice.

Pound shallots, garlic, white pepper, dried shrimp, and shrimp paste into a paste. Bring water or stock to a boil and dissolve the paste into it. Add firm vegetables first, such as squash and corn, followed by mushrooms and leafy greens. Add shrimp and cook until just done. Season with fish sauce. Finish with Thai basil or lemon basil and serve hot.

Boil the eggs until cooked, then peel them. Heat oil and fry the boiled eggs until the outside becomes golden, chewy, and slightly crisp. Remove and drain. Fry sliced shallots and garlic until crisp, then set aside. Prepare the sweet-sour tamarind sauce by simmering tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, and a little water until glossy and balanced. Halve the fried eggs and arrange them on a plate. Spoon the tamarind sauce over the eggs and top with crispy fried shallots, crispy garlic, chilies if using, and fresh coriander.
searchAliases: thai fried eggs with tamarind sauce

strInstructions: Simmer pork ribs in water with lemongrass, galangal, makrut lime leaves, shallots, and chilies until the pork is tender and the broth is aromatic. Season with fish sauce, lime juice, and roasted chili flakes or chili powder. Add herbs such as cilantro, green onions, and sawtooth coriander. Taste for a bold balance of sour, salty, spicy, and savory. Serve hot with rice.

Prepare a clear pork or chicken broth. Mix red fermented tofu, vinegar, sugar, chili sauce, and garlic to make the pink yen ta fo sauce. Cook rice noodles, fish balls, fried tofu, squid, shrimp, and water spinach separately or in the broth until done. Place noodles in bowls, add seafood and toppings, then ladle broth over them. Add yen ta fo sauce and serve with chili vinegar.

Pound garlic and coriander roots together with a mortar and pestle or food processor. Heat oil in a pan over medium heat and fry the garlic-coriander paste until fragrant and golden. Add white pepper and uncooked jasmine rice, then stir for about 30 seconds until the rice is lightly coated and toasted. In a large soup pot, bring water to a simmer. Add the bouillon cube, salt, and light soy sauce, stirring until dissolved. Add the toasted rice mixture and simmer until the rice softens into soup. Roll the ground pork into small meatballs and add them to the soup. Cook until the pork is done. Stir in celery, then garnish with chopped green onions and crispy fried shallots if desired. Serve hot.
strMeasure11: 7 oz or 1 cup, rolled into meatballs

strInstructions: Bring stock to a simmer in a saucepan. Add cooked rice and simmer until the grains soften and the soup thickens slightly. Add pork mince or pork balls and cook until done. Season with fish sauce or soy sauce. Ladle into bowls and top with ginger, green onion, coriander, fried garlic, white pepper, and a soft egg if desired. Serve hot for breakfast or a light meal.

Rinse the glutinous rice several times until the water runs mostly clear, then soak it for at least 4 hours or overnight. Drain the rice and steam it until tender and chewy. While the rice is steaming, heat coconut milk with sugar and salt until the sugar dissolves, but do not boil hard. When the rice is cooked, transfer it to a bowl and gradually mix in the sweetened coconut milk. Cover and let it rest until the rice absorbs the coconut mixture. For the topping sauce, simmer coconut milk with sugar, salt, and a little rice flour or cornstarch slurry until slightly thickened. Serve the coconut sticky rice with sliced ripe mango, drizzle with the coconut sauce, and sprinkle with toasted mung beans or sesame seeds.
strMeasure2: 1 cup for rice plus 1/2 cup for sauce
strMeasure3: 1/2 cup for rice plus 2 tbsp for sauce
strMeasure4: 1/2 tsp for rice plus 1/4 tsp for sauce
strMeasure5: 1 tsp mixed with a little water
strIngredient7: Toasted Mung Beans or Sesame Seeds
